items.passage:
Photography, the practice of recording images with light-sensitive materials, emerged as a chemical novelty in the early nineteenth century. Its earliest form, the daguerreotype, fixed a single detailed image on a polished silver plate. [■] These plates were fragile and could not be copied, so each picture remained unique. Yet the process quickly captured the public imagination across Europe and America.

This enthusiasm soon drove inventors to seek cheaper and more flexible methods. [■] The negative-positive system, developed first on paper and later on glass, allowed a single exposure to yield many separate prints. Because copies became easy to make, photographs spread far beyond wealthy collectors. [■] Commercial portrait studios then grew rapidly during the following decades.

This commercial expansion gradually reshaped how ordinary people pictured themselves. Families who had never owned a painting could now display accurate likenesses on their walls. [■] Photography also began to document distant places, wars, and scientific subjects for a curious public. However, its claim to perfect truth was questioned almost immediately, since photographers may stage scenes and retouch images to change what viewers actually see.
paragraphs: Photography, the practice of recording images with light-sensitive materials, emerged as a chemical novelty in the early nineteenth century. Its earliest form, the daguerreotype, fixed a single detailed image on a polished silver plate. [■] These plates were fragile and could not be copied, so each picture remained unique. Yet the process quickly captured the public imagination across Europe and America.

This enthusiasm soon drove inventors to seek cheaper and more flexible methods. [■] The negative-positive system, developed first on paper and later on glass, allowed a single exposure to yield many separate prints. Because copies became easy to make, photographs spread far beyond wealthy collectors. [■] Commercial portrait studios then grew rapidly during the following decades.

This commercial expansion gradually reshaped how ordinary people pictured themselves. Families who had never owned a painting could now display accurate likenesses on their walls. [■] Photography also began to document distant places, wars, and scientific subjects for a curious public. However, its claim to perfect truth was questioned almost immediately, since photographers may stage scenes and retouch images to change what viewers actually see.

questions.stem: The word 'novelty' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: tradition
options.B: hardship
options.C: decoration
options.D: innovation

questions.explanation: The passage frames photography as a 'chemical novelty' that had just emerged, so 'novelty' here means a new invention, matching 'innovation.'

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what did the negative-positive system make possible?

options.A: It fixed a single image on a silver plate
options.B: It produced many prints from one exposure
options.C: It removed the need for portrait studios
options.D: It made photographic plates more fragile

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states the negative-positive system 'allowed a single exposure to yield many separate prints.'

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about early photographs?

options.A: They could never record distant historical events
options.B: They were once trusted as truthful records
options.C: They were used only by trained scientists
options.D: They quickly replaced all painting

questions.explanation: The passage says photography's 'claim to perfect truth was soon questioned,' which implies that people had earlier accepted photographs as accurate, truthful records.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention families 'who had never owned a painting' in paragraph 3?

options.A: To illustrate how photography reached ordinary households
options.B: To argue that paintings had lost all their value
options.C: To explain the chemistry of early photographic plates
options.D: To describe one particular famous portrait studio

questions.explanation: The families who could 'now display accurate likenesses on their walls' serve as an example of how photography spread to ordinary people, not just the wealthy.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, inexpensive card-mounted portraits known as cartes de visite were soon traded among friends by the millions.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1 (paragraph 1)
options.B: Location 2 (paragraph 2)
options.C: Location 3 (paragraph 2)
options.D: Location 4 (paragraph 3)

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a concrete example of copies spreading widely, so it fits best right after 'photographs spread far beyond wealthy collectors' and before the mention of booming commercial studios (Location 3).

items.passage:
Fossilization, the process by which the remains of organisms become preserved in rock, captures only a small fraction of the life that once existed. Hard parts such as bones and shells survive readily, while soft tissues decay long before mineral replacement can occur. [■] As a result, the fossil record is heavily biased toward durable body parts and shallow marine settings. Whole groups of soft-bodied creatures therefore leave almost no trace at all.

This gap has been narrowed by rare deposits known as Lagerstätten, where unusual conditions halt normal decay. [■] Fine sediment and very low oxygen can preserve delicate features such as feathers, skin, and internal organs. Modern scanning methods then reveal structures hidden inside the surrounding rock without destroying the fragile specimen itself.

These exceptional windows have transformed how scientists picture ancient ecosystems. [■] Detailed soft-tissue evidence allows researchers to reconstruct diet, movement, and coloration once thought completely unrecoverable. Such deposits occasionally preserve an animal's last meal still resting inside its gut. [■] However, Lagerstätten remain rare and geographically scattered, so gaps in the record may never be entirely closed.
paragraphs: Fossilization, the process by which the remains of organisms become preserved in rock, captures only a small fraction of the life that once existed. Hard parts such as bones and shells survive readily, while soft tissues decay long before mineral replacement can occur. [■] As a result, the fossil record is heavily biased toward durable body parts and shallow marine settings. Whole groups of soft-bodied creatures therefore leave almost no trace at all.

This gap has been narrowed by rare deposits known as Lagerstätten, where unusual conditions halt normal decay. [■] Fine sediment and very low oxygen can preserve delicate features such as feathers, skin, and internal organs. Modern scanning methods then reveal structures hidden inside the surrounding rock without destroying the fragile specimen itself.

These exceptional windows have transformed how scientists picture ancient ecosystems. [■] Detailed soft-tissue evidence allows researchers to reconstruct diet, movement, and coloration once thought completely unrecoverable. Such deposits occasionally preserve an animal's last meal still resting inside its gut. [■] However, Lagerstätten remain rare and geographically scattered, so gaps in the record may never be entirely closed.

questions.stem: The word 'halt' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: measure
options.B: spread
options.C: record
options.D: stop

questions.explanation: Lagerstätten are places 'where unusual conditions halt normal decay,' so 'halt' means to bring to a stop.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how do Lagerstätten preserve delicate features?

options.A: By replacing soft tissue with solid bone
options.B: By exposing the specimens to open air
options.C: Through fine sediment and low oxygen
options.D: Through the rapid heating of the rock

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that 'fine sediment and very low oxygen can preserve delicate features such as feathers, skin, and internal organs.'

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: Soft tissues survive more easily than hard bones
options.B: Hard parts such as shells preserve quite readily
options.C: Lagerstätten can preserve feathers and skin
options.D: Scanning can reveal structures hidden in rock

questions.explanation: The passage says the opposite: 'Hard parts such as bones and shells survive readily, while soft tissues decay long before mineral replacement can occur.'

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about the fossil record?

options.A: It is now regarded as essentially complete
options.B: It preserves mostly delicate soft tissue
options.C: It is limited entirely to marine animals
options.D: It will probably stay incomplete despite advances

questions.explanation: Because 'Lagerstätten remain rare and geographically scattered, so gaps in the record may never be entirely closed,' the record is likely to stay incomplete even as methods improve.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For instance, the Burgess Shale in Canada has yielded thousands of soft-bodied animals unknown from any other site.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1 (paragraph 1)
options.B: Location 2 (paragraph 2)
options.C: Location 3 (paragraph 3)
options.D: Location 4 (paragraph 3)

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a specific example of a Lagerstätte, so it fits best right after Lagerstätten are introduced as rare deposits that halt decay (Location 2).

items.passage:
The placebo effect, a genuine improvement in symptoms following an inactive treatment, has long puzzled physicians. In earlier centuries, healers unknowingly relied on it, offering remedies that worked mainly through a patient's expectation of relief. [■] Such responses were often dismissed as mere imagination rather than treated as real medical effects.

This attitude changed once controlled trials became standard practice in the twentieth century. [■] Researchers began comparing active drugs against inert pills to separate true chemical action from simple belief. Because many patients improved on the dummy pills alone, the placebo response could no longer be ignored by serious science. Instead, it became a phenomenon that demanded a proper physiological explanation.

Recent neuroscience has traced this response to measurable activity in the brain. [■] Expectation of relief can trigger the release of natural painkilling chemicals, producing effects that brain scanners detect directly. Similar mechanisms appear to influence responses to fatigue, anxiety, and even certain forms of pain. [■] However, the placebo effect varies widely between individuals, and its underlying mechanisms may never be fully mapped.
paragraphs: The placebo effect, a genuine improvement in symptoms following an inactive treatment, has long puzzled physicians. In earlier centuries, healers unknowingly relied on it, offering remedies that worked mainly through a patient's expectation of relief. [■] Such responses were often dismissed as mere imagination rather than treated as real medical effects.

This attitude changed once controlled trials became standard practice in the twentieth century. [■] Researchers began comparing active drugs against inert pills to separate true chemical action from simple belief. Because many patients improved on the dummy pills alone, the placebo response could no longer be ignored by serious science. Instead, it became a phenomenon that demanded a proper physiological explanation.

Recent neuroscience has traced this response to measurable activity in the brain. [■] Expectation of relief can trigger the release of natural painkilling chemicals, producing effects that brain scanners detect directly. Similar mechanisms appear to influence responses to fatigue, anxiety, and even certain forms of pain. [■] However, the placebo effect varies widely between individuals, and its underlying mechanisms may never be fully mapped.

questions.stem: The word 'inert' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: harmful
options.B: expensive
options.C: inactive
options.D: natural

questions.explanation: The 'inert pills' are contrasted with 'active drugs' and later called 'dummy pills,' so 'inert' means inactive.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why did the remedies of early healers sometimes work?

options.A: Patients expected the treatment to help
options.B: The remedies contained active healing ingredients
options.C: Healers relied on precise measurements
options.D: Symptoms faded on their own

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says the remedies 'worked mainly through a patient's expectation of relief.'

questions.stem: What does 'This attitude' refer to at the start of paragraph 2?

options.A: the dismissal of placebo responses as imagination
options.B: the use of controlled clinical trials
options.C: the release of natural painkilling chemicals
options.D: the expectation of relief in patients

questions.explanation: The end of paragraph 1 states that 'such responses were often dismissed as mere imagination,' which is the attitude that later changed.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about modern views of the placebo effect?

options.A: It is now considered entirely imaginary
options.B: It produces the same response in everyone
options.C: It has been fully and completely explained
options.D: It reflects real, measurable brain activity

questions.explanation: Because neuroscience has 'traced this response to measurable activity in the brain' that 'scanners detect directly,' the effect is now understood as a real, physical process.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For instance, the double-blind method ensured that neither the patients nor their doctors knew who received the real medicine.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1 (paragraph 1)
options.B: Location 2 (paragraph 2)
options.C: Location 3 (paragraph 3)
options.D: Location 4 (paragraph 3)

questions.explanation: The sentence details a specific feature of controlled trials, so it fits best right after such trials 'became standard practice' and before researchers compare drugs against inert pills (Location 2).

items.passage:
Trade networks, the linked routes along which goods and information travel between distant regions, have shaped economies since ancient times. Along these routes, merchants exchanged not only physical products but also technologies, customs, and ideas. The most successful networks connected many separate markets, allowing scarce goods to reach buyers far from their original source.

These connections depended on a set of practical institutions. Middlemen bought goods in one market and resold them in another, smoothing differences in local supply and demand. Because carrying heavy coins over long distances was risky, traders developed letters of credit that could be exchanged for cash at distant ports. Such tools lowered costs and encouraged still wider exchange between regions.

Beyond simply moving goods, trade networks often reshaped the societies they linked together. Cities that were positioned at major crossings grew wealthy by taxing and storing passing cargo. The steady contact between regions spread languages and beliefs alongside ordinary merchandise. However, the same routes could also carry disease and conflict, so their expansion sometimes brought serious risks as well.
paragraphs: Trade networks, the linked routes along which goods and information travel between distant regions, have shaped economies since ancient times. Along these routes, merchants exchanged not only physical products but also technologies, customs, and ideas. The most successful networks connected many separate markets, allowing scarce goods to reach buyers far from their original source.

These connections depended on a set of practical institutions. Middlemen bought goods in one market and resold them in another, smoothing differences in local supply and demand. Because carrying heavy coins over long distances was risky, traders developed letters of credit that could be exchanged for cash at distant ports. Such tools lowered costs and encouraged still wider exchange between regions.

Beyond simply moving goods, trade networks often reshaped the societies they linked together. Cities that were positioned at major crossings grew wealthy by taxing and storing passing cargo. The steady contact between regions spread languages and beliefs alongside ordinary merchandise. However, the same routes could also carry disease and conflict, so their expansion sometimes brought serious risks as well.

questions.stem: The word 'scarce' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: rare
options.B: cheap
options.C: heavy
options.D: local

questions.explanation: The passage describes goods that had to travel far to 'reach buyers far from their original source,' so 'scarce' means rare or hard to obtain.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why did traders develop letters of credit?

options.A: Carrying coins over long distances was risky
options.B: Middlemen everywhere refused to accept coins
options.C: Distant markets had no real demand
options.D: Rulers had banned the use of cash

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains that 'because carrying heavy coins over long distances was risky, traders developed letters of credit.'

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about middlemen?

options.A: They produced most goods themselves
options.B: They profited from differences in market prices
options.C: They traded only within a single city
options.D: They replaced credit letters with coins

questions.explanation: Middlemen 'bought goods in one market and resold them in another,' so they earned money from the price gaps between those markets.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention letters of credit in paragraph 2?

options.A: To criticize the greed of early merchants
options.B: To describe how ancient coins were made
options.C: To show how traders reduced exchange risks
options.D: To explain a single city's tax system

questions.explanation: Letters of credit are offered as an example of a 'practical institution' that let traders avoid the danger of carrying coins, reducing the risks of exchange.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: Trade networks carried ideas as well as goods
options.B: Middlemen helped smooth supply and demand
options.C: Some cities grew wealthy from passing cargo
options.D: Trade routes never spread disease or conflict

questions.explanation: The passage states the opposite: 'the same routes could also carry disease and conflict.'

items.passage:
Wildlife corridors, strips of protected habitat that connect otherwise isolated natural areas, help animals move safely across landscapes fragmented by human development. As farms, roads, and cities expand, many populations become trapped in shrinking patches of green. Corridors aim to restore the connections that once let species roam freely between regions.

This linking function supports wildlife in several practical ways. By joining separate populations, corridors allow individuals to mate across groups, which maintains genetic diversity and reduces harmful inbreeding. They also let animals follow seasonal food and water and shift their ranges as the climate steadily warms. Predators and pollinators alike benefit from the wider territory these paths provide.

Despite these clear advantages, designing effective corridors remains surprisingly difficult. Planners must guess which routes animals will actually use, and a poorly placed path may attract poachers or invasive species. Land near cities is also expensive, so corridors often compete with housing and agriculture for space. Careful monitoring is required to confirm that a corridor truly serves the species it targets.
paragraphs: Wildlife corridors, strips of protected habitat that connect otherwise isolated natural areas, help animals move safely across landscapes fragmented by human development. As farms, roads, and cities expand, many populations become trapped in shrinking patches of green. Corridors aim to restore the connections that once let species roam freely between regions.

This linking function supports wildlife in several practical ways. By joining separate populations, corridors allow individuals to mate across groups, which maintains genetic diversity and reduces harmful inbreeding. They also let animals follow seasonal food and water and shift their ranges as the climate steadily warms. Predators and pollinators alike benefit from the wider territory these paths provide.

Despite these clear advantages, designing effective corridors remains surprisingly difficult. Planners must guess which routes animals will actually use, and a poorly placed path may attract poachers or invasive species. Land near cities is also expensive, so corridors often compete with housing and agriculture for space. Careful monitoring is required to confirm that a corridor truly serves the species it targets.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: Why animal populations decline in and around large cities
options.B: How corridors connect habitats and prove difficult to design
options.C: The genetic effects of inbreeding within small populations
options.D: The rising price of land near growing cities

questions.explanation: The passage defines corridors, describes how they help wildlife, and then explains why they are hard to design, covering both their function and their challenges.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how do corridors help maintain genetic diversity?

options.A: By removing predators from the habitat
options.B: By letting separate populations mate together
options.C: By providing extra food during the winter
options.D: By blocking the movement of invasive species

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says corridors 'allow individuals to mate across groups, which maintains genetic diversity and reduces harmful inbreeding.'

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraph 2?

options.A: It presents challenges that complicate the benefits described earlier
options.B: It repeats the same benefits in much greater detail
options.C: It gives the historical origin of wildlife corridors
options.D: It rejects the idea that corridors work at all

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 lists the advantages of corridors, while paragraph 3 opens with 'Despite these clear advantages' and turns to the difficulties of designing them.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about corridor planning?

options.A: Corridors always help every nearby wild species
options.B: Animal routes are simple to predict reliably
options.C: Corridors cost much less than other conservation methods
options.D: Poorly planned corridors can fail their target species

questions.explanation: Since planners 'must guess which routes animals will actually use' and a 'poorly placed path' can attract threats, a badly designed corridor may not help the species it targets.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what makes building corridors near cities difficult?

options.A: City rules forbid new conservation projects nearby
options.B: Wild animals completely refuse to enter urban areas
options.C: Land is costly and competes with other uses
options.D: Corridors attract far too many curious tourists

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 notes that 'land near cities is also expensive, so corridors often compete with housing and agriculture for space.'
